question: What bases/platforms does charmcraft support and how do you specify multi-base builds?

claude_output.result:
## Charmcraft Bases & Platforms

Charmcraft supports several Ubuntu releases and architectures, and offers two syntaxes for specifying them.

---

### Supported Bases

| **Ubuntu Releases** | **Architectures** |
|---|---|
| `ubuntu@22.04` (Jammy) | `amd64` (x86-64) |
| `ubuntu@24.04` (Noble) | `arm64` (ARMv8) |
| `ubuntu@24.10` (Oracular) | `armhf` (ARMv7) |
| `ubuntu@25.04` (Plucky) | `ppc64el` (POWER) |
| `almalinux@9` | `s390x` (IBM Z) |
| `devel` (build-base only) | `riscv64` (RISC-V) |

---

### Single-Base Charm (Simple Case)

When your charm targets only one base, use a top-level `base` key:

```yaml
base: ubuntu@24.04
platforms:
  amd64:
  arm64:
```

---

### Multi-Base Charm

When your charm needs to support **multiple Ubuntu series**, omit the top-level `base` and specify it **per-platform** instead:

```yaml
platforms:
  ubuntu-22-04-amd64:
    base: ubuntu@22.04
    build-on: [amd64]
    build-for: [amd64]
  ubuntu-24-04-amd64:
    base: ubuntu@24.04
    build-on: [amd64]
    build-for: [amd64]
  ubuntu-24-04-arm64:
    base: ubuntu@24.04
    build-on: [amd64, arm64]
    build-for: [arm64]
```

Each platform entry has an **arbitrary name** (e.g. `ubuntu-24-04-arm64`) and its own `base`, `build-on`, and `build-for`.

---

### `build-on` vs `build-for`

| Key | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **`build-on`** | Architecture(s) of the **build host** | `[amd64]` — build on x86-64 machines |
| **`build-for`** | Architecture of the **target runtime** | `[arm64]` — the charm runs on ARM64 |

This enables **cross-compilation**, e.g. building an ARM64 charm on an amd64 host:

```yaml
platforms:
  cross-arm64:
    base: ubuntu@24.04
    build-on: [amd64]       # build host
    build-for: [arm64]      # target runtime
```

---

### Old `bases` Syntax (Deprecated)

The older syntax used a top-level `bases` key with nested `build-on`/`run-on` lists. This is **deprecated** in favor of the `base` + `platforms` approach shown above. If you're starting a new charm, use the `platforms` syntax.
